IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST
REPUBLIC OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal made under
Section 331(1) of the Code of Criminal
Procedure Act No.15 of 1979 read with
Article 138 of the Constitution of the
Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri
Lanka.
Court of Appeal Case No. Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri
CA/HCC/0117-118/2023 Lanka
High Court of Balapitiya
Case No. Cri/1131/2008 COMPLAINANT
Vs.
1. Thuiah Handi Manoj Hasintha 2. Sangama Chanaka Jayawardena
ACCUSED
AND NOW BETWEEN
1. Thuiah Handi Manoj Hasintha 2. Sangama Chanaka Jayawardena
ACCUSED-APPELLANTS
Vs.
2 | P a g e
The Hon. Attorney General
Attorney General's Department
Colombo-12
COMPLAINANT-RESPONDENT
BEFORE : P. Kumararatnam, J.
R. P. Hettiarachchi, J.
COUNSEL : Harshana Ananda for the Appellant.
Dishna Warnakula, DSG for the
Respondent.
ARGUED ON : 23/02/2026
DECIDED ON : 02/07/2026
3 | P a g e
JUDGMENT
P. Kumararatnam. J,
The above-named Accused-Appellants (hereinafter referred to as the
Appellants) were indicted for committing the murder of
Dadumathumullagamage Lishan Maduranga on 23.09.2005 which is an
offence punishable under Section 296 of the Penal Code.
After a non-jury trial, the Learned High Court Judge has found the
Appellants guilty of the charge and sentenced them to death on 03/03/2023.
The prosecution had called seven witnesses and marked productions at the
trial. As the Appellants were tried in absentia the defence called three
witnesses on behalf of them and closed their case.
Being aggrieved by the aforesaid conviction and sentence, the Appellants
preferred this appeal to this court.
At the hearing of this appeal, only the 2nd Appellant was represented by a
Counsel. The appeal of the 1st Appellant was not argued.
History of the case
When this case was called on 18.11.2008 for service of indictment, only the
1st Appellant was present and indictment was only served to him and the
plea was recorded. The Appellant pleaded not guilty to the charge and elected
a non-jury trial.
While the prosecution was preparing to lead evidence under Section 241(1)
of the Criminal Procedure Act No.15 of 1979 (Hereinafter referred to as the
4 | P a g e
CPC) in respect of the 2nd Appellant, the 1st Appellant also absconded court.
Hence, court ordered the prosecution to take steps under Section 241(1) of
the CPC.
On 24.04.2013 the 2nd Appellant was produced to court and was remanded
by the Learned High Court Judge. 241(1) inquiry was held in respect of the
1st Appellant and the case was fixed in absentia of him. On 01.03.2016, the
Learned High Court Judge had commenced the trial stating that the
indictments had been served on the Appellants and explained the right of a
jury trial. The relevant portion of the proceeding is re-produced below:
Page 197 of the brief.
pQos;hska yg wêfpdaokd lshjd oS" wêfpdaokd m;% Ndr oS we;s w;r" cQrs iNdjla bosrsfha
úNd.hg .ekSug we;s whs;sh o myod oS we;'
ta wkqj kvqj úNd.hg kshñ; j we;'
The Appellants had been properly represented by their Counsel during the
trial, even though the trial proceeded against them in absentia. At the
hearing of this appeal, the 1st Appellant was not represented by a Counsel.
Only the Counsel for the 2nd Appellant argued the matter confining his
argument only in respect of the 2nd Appellant. No argument was placed on
behalf of the 1st Appellant.
An appellate court cannot merely dismiss a criminal appeal in situations
where there is lack of prosecution or where the appellant fails to pursue it.
Considering the liberty and legal rights that are at stake, the Court would
legally be required assess the trial court’s record and decide the case based
on its merits. As such, it cannot merely be dismissed based on the absence
or inactivity of the appellant.
Therefore, this Court has a bounden duty to consider the 1st Appellant’s case.
Hence, I will consider the Appeal of the 1st Appellant first.
5 | P a g e
The 1st Appellant had preferred the following grounds of Appeal in the
Petition of Appeal. The grounds are re-produced below:
1. The judgment of the Learned High Court Judge is illegal and contrary
to the law.
2. The contradictions and omissions marked in the prosecution case were
not taken into consideration when analysing the evidence.
3. The Learned High Court Judge has failed to properly analyse the
defence evidence.
4. Due consideration was not given to the fact that the prosecution has
failed to prove their case beyond a reasonable doubt.
5. The evidence tendered throughout the case was not analysed by the
Learned High Court Judge with a judicial mind.
6. The evidence tendered in favour of the defence has been ignored by the
Learned High Court Judge in arriving at his judgment.
7. The judgement of the Learned High Court Judge has failed to give due
consideration to the fact that the indictment was not served to the 2nd
accused at the beginning of the trial.
8. Even though the Learned High Court Judge had accepted the fact that
he has not satisfied with how the trial has been conducted, he has
ignored that fact and given his judgement.
9. The prosecution has failed to call any investigation officer or police
officer as a witness to their case and the Learned High Court Judge
has failed to go through the contradictions marked in the testimony of
the only police officer called by the prosecution, with a judicial mind.
10. Due weightage was not given to the defence of alibi taken by the
Accused-Appellants from the beginning of the trial, when analysing the
defence evidence.
11. When there were certain defects identified in the post mortem
report and the defence witness No. 3 has been able to create a
reasonable doubt in the testimony of PW7, the Learned High Court
Judge has failed to analyse those facts with a judicial mind.
6 | P a g e
The 2nd Appellant has raised the following grounds of appeal in support
his argument:
1. The Learned High Court Judge erred in law by failing to appreciate
that fact that the indictment not being read over and jury option was
not given to the 2nd Appellant have caused a grave miscarriage of
justice in respect of the 2nd Appellant.
2. 2nd Appellant has not been afforded a fair trial.
3. Learned High Court Judge erred in law holding that the prosecution
has proved the case beyond reasonable doubt.
4. The Learned High Court judge has unreasonably rejected the
contradictions marked by the defence.
Background of the Case
According to PW1 Amarasiri, the father of the deceased, the deceased had
gone to the ‘Paraliya’ play ground to play cricket. About half an hour after
his departure, he had received a phone call from PW2, saying that his son
had been shot and he had rushed to the playground. He had lodged the first
complaint with the Meetiyagoda Police Station.
PW4 Maduranga had seen the incident when walking towards the “Paraliya”
ground from the Paraliya junction. The Appellants had arrived at the scene
in a bicycle ridden by the 1st Appellant. The 2nd Appellant who was sitting on
the front bar, had alighted from the bicycle, and had gone up to the deceased
who was fielding near the boundary line. He had opened fire twice and
threatened the others not to give evidence, and both Appellants had ridden
the bicycle away from the scene.
PW2, Manohara, too said that while playing cricket, the 2nd Appellant had
come with the 1st Appellant and had shot the deceased to death.
According to PW7 the JMO had observed four injuries on the deceased’s
body. The fatal injury was observed on the left side of the head. The 3rd injury
was on the left upper arm and the 2nd injury was the corresponding exit
7 | P a g e
wound of 3rd. The cause of death was severe brain injuries due to discharge
of a firearm (Lacerated left cerebellar lobe and left temporal of the brain).
PW10, PS 29245 Livera gave evidence with regard to the investigation.
As the Learned High Court called the defence, the Appellants called three
witnesses. The first two witnesses, including DW01 Sumathirathne, testified
that the Appellants were engaged in roofing the house of the 1st Appellant.
Defence witness Professor Perera (DW04) testified that the fact that no
burning, blackening, or tattooing around the wounds indicate that the shots
may have been fired at a distance of about 04-05 feet. Further, he opined
that there could be tears on the upper cloth if the deceased had worn
anything at the time of shooting.
As the Appeal grounds raised on behalf of the 1st Appellant are
interconnected, all grounds will be considered together hereinafter.
PW2 and PW4 are eye witnesses to the incident. They had witnessed the
incident while they were playing cricket along with the deceased. Both had
very clearly seen the incident and identified the Appellants perfectly.
In a criminal case, two eyewitnesses being available would greatly strengthen
the prosecution’s case, which can be done through the corroboration of
timelines and identification of suspects. In such cases, judges and juries
would be compelled by such direct eyewitness testimony. As such, two
witnesses that agree on the same details would usually secure higher
conviction judgments, as opposed to cases with merely one single witness.
In the case of Kumara de Silva & Others V Attorney General (2010) 2 Sri
L.R. 169, it was held that;
"Credibility is a question of fact, not of law. Appeal Court Judges
repeatedly stress the importance of the trial judge's observations of the
demeanour of witnesses in deciding questions of fact. The acceptance or
rejection of evidence of witnesses is therefore a question of fact for the trial
8 | P a g e
Judge, since he or she is in the best position to hear and observe
witnesses. In such a situation the Appellate courts will be slow to interfere
with the findings of the trial Judge unless such evidence could be shown
to be totally inconsistent or perverse and lacking credibility."
In Ariyadasa v Attorney-General, (2012) 1 Sri. L.R. 84], Sisira de Abrew, J
held:
“Court of Appeal will not lightly disturb a finding of a trial Judge with
regard to the acceptance or rejection of a testimony of a witness, unless
it is manifestly wrong, when the trial judge has taken such a decision
after observing the demeanour and the deportment of a witness. This is
because the trial Judge has the priceless advantage to observe the
demeanour and deportment of the witness which the Court of Appeal
does not have.”
This position was analysed in the case of Veerasamy Sivathasan v.
Honourable Attorney General [2021] 2 SLR 290 (SC Appeal 208/2012) as
follows:
“One key reason for the afore-stated approach is that the trial court has
the invaluable advantage of observing the demeanour and deportment of
witnesses who testify at the trial, and using such observations for the
purpose of assessing and determining the credibility of witnesses.
Furthermore, the gradual and incremental unfolding of human testimony
and other evidence during the course of a trial, and the ability of the trial
judge to question witnesses and obtain clarifications, place the trial judge
in an advantageous position in appreciating the evidence and the
respective positions of the prosecution and the defence. These advantages
are not available to an appellate Court, which is called upon to determine
the lawfulness of the finding of the trial Court, based on a consideration of
the verbatim transcript of the trial proceedings together with the judgment
9 | P a g e
of the trial judge, aided by submissions of counsel and the reasons for the
findings contained in the judgment.”
As per the evidence given by PW2 and PW4, it has clearly been established
that the Appellants acted together to commit the murder of the deceased by
shooting. In order to establish common intention, it is necessary to prove a
shared meeting of minds, where mere accidental presence or independent
actions would not suffice. Courts would infer this by considering the
circumstantial and direct evidence available.
In this case the witnesses clearly outlined how the Appellants arrived at the
scene of crime with coordination and possession of a coordinated weapon.
After shooting the deceased, it is evident that the Appellants had left the
place after threatening not to give evidence to the people who were there at
that time. This clearly shows their participatory presence.
In SC Appeal 130/2014 dated 14.09.2022 Kodagoda, J. held that:
Therefore, it needs to be recognised that the principle of ‘common intention’
is a means by which criminal liability is (a) shared between co-participants
in the commission of the crime on the basis of their having shared a
common intention, and committed certain actions in furtherance of such
common intention, or (b) extended to a person who may not be the principal
offender responsible directly for commission of the offence, on the basis of
such other person having entertained a common intention together with the
principal offender and such other person having acted in furtherance of the
common intention with the principal offender. The same concept would
apply to similar situations involving more than two persons.
In Gunasiri and others v Republic [2009] 1 SLR 39 it was held that:
“To establish the existence of common intention it is not essential to
prove that the criminal act was done pursuant to a pre-arranged plan.
10 | P a g e
A common intention can come into existence without a prearrangement.
It can be formed on the spur of the movement.”
In the case of The Queen v Vincent Fernando and two others (1963) 65
NLR 265, (at p. 272) it was held that:
“a person who merely shares the criminal intention or takes a fiendish
delight in what is happening but does no criminal act in furtherance of the
common intention of all is not liable for the acts of the others. To be liable
under section 32 a mental sharing of the common intention is not sufficient,
the sharing must be evidenced by a criminal act.
… where a series of criminal acts is done by several persons, each act
would be done either jointly or severally. But whether the criminal acts in
the series of criminal acts are done jointly or severally if each criminal act
is done in furtherance of the common intention of all each of the persons
sharing the common intention and doing any act in the series of criminal
acts is not only liable for his own act but is also liable for the acts of the
others in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.”
The Learned High Court Judge had very correctly analysed the evidence
presented by the prosecution and held that this is a premeditated murder
considering the participation of the Appellants in committing the murder of
the deceased.
The defence witnesses are the uncle and the mother of the 1st Appellant and
the Learned High Court Judge had considered their evidence and given
reasons as to why he disregarded their evidence.
In this case, the Appellant was properly arraigned, the indictment was read
out and jury option was given to him. He had chosen that his case be tried
before a judge. He was properly represented unto filing his Petition of Appeal.
Hence, no prejudice has been caused to him even though he was tried in
absentia due to his continuous absence in Court.
11 | P a g e
In this case, PW2 and PW4 had clearly witnessed the incident and described
how the murder was committed by the Appellants. They had given cogent
and convincing evidence.
Considering the evidence given by PW2 and PW4, and other witnesses,
finding the Appellants guilty of the charge has not caused any prejudice to
the Appellants. Therefore, I affirm the conviction in respect of the 1st
Appellant and dismiss his appeal.
In respect of the second Appellant the Learned Counsel strenuously
contended that his client was deprived of a fair trial. His argument was that
the 2nd Appellant was not properly arraigned in this case. The charge was
not read to him and jury option was not given. The Learned High Court Judge
too admitted but he failed to take preventive measures before he could
pronounce the judgment. Instead, he had commented in his judgement. The
relevant portion is re-produced below:
Page 522 of the brief.

As correctly argued, the Learned High Court Judge has tried to justify this
lapse on the basis that the second Appellant was represented by a Counsel
12 | P a g e
In criminal trials, a charge refers to the formal, written accusation by the
government stating that the individual has committed a specific crime. A
charge would serve as the foundation of the legal proceedings that follow,
and therefore would serve as the blueprint which determines factors such as
the relevance of evidence, what must be proven by the prosecution and the
defendant’s rights.
As such, if a criminal trial is concluded with a judgment being delivered,
where the accused was never read or explained the charges, such a judgment
or conviction would be invalid. The rationale for this is that failure to formally
read the charges would violate fundamental legal rights and lead to a
miscarriage of justice.
In Sri Lanka’s criminal courts, trial by jury is an option which is primarily
available for the most serious crimes heard in the High Court. As per the
Judicature Act, an accused would have the right to elect whether to be tried
by a jury or by a judge sitting alone.
In Attorney General v Segulebbe Latheef and Another [2008] 1 SLR 225
the Supreme Court held that:
“As long as it is in the statute book that the accused can elect to be tried
by a jury, the trial judge has an obligation not only to inquire from him
whether he is to be tried by a jury, judge must also inform that the accused
has a legal right to that effect. Non observance of this procedure is an
illegality and not a mere irregularity”.
In Jayasooriya and others v AG [2009] 1 SLR 101 the Court held that:
"To ensure a fair trial, the legislature in its wisdom from time to time has
promulgated several fundamental concepts and statutory duties into our
criminal law, the offering of the jury option is one such concept".
Appellate courts would therefore consider such omissions to be fatal
procedural defects rather than merely minor technicalities. Therefore, the
13 | P a g e
entire trial process may be declared void. In situations where the charge has
not been properly read and the process has not been followed; the Appellate
Court has the power to order a fresh trial.
Guided by the judgements mentioned above, I conclude that this is an
appropriate case to send for re-trial in respect of the 2nd Appellant. Therefore,
his appeal allowed.
The Registrar of this Court is directed to send this judgment to High Court
of Balapitiya along with the original case record.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
R. P. Hettiarachchi, J.
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
